Biblical figures

Historical figures

Mythological figures

Contemporaries of Dante

The first circle of Inferno is the home of Virgil himself, as well as many other ancient Greek and Roman scholars.

Upon asking Virgil if anyone has ever left Limbo, Dante is informed that a powerful being (Jesus Christ) came down and retrieved many Biblical figures (particularly the patriarchs), such as Adam, Abel, Noah, Moses, Abraham, David, Isaac, Israel (Jacob), his sons (the progenitors of the Twelve Tribes of Israel), and his second wife, Rachel.

Note: Quotation displayed on main view is from Canto III, which is before Dante actually enters Limbo.

Before entering the Second Circle, all sinners must confess to the mythological king of Crete, Minos. Once he has decided to which circle a soul will be condemned, he wraps his serpentine tail around himself the number of times that corresponds to the circle of the Inferno.

The lustful sinners sentenced to the Second Circle are endlessly pummeled by a vicious windstorm, battering them without rest.

In the Circle of Gluttony, the gluttons are condemned to grovel in vile slush and mud. English novelist Dorothy L. Sayers wrote that "the surrender to sin which began with mutual indulgence leads by an imperceptible degradation to solitary self-indulgence," in her notes on Canto VI while translating The Divine Comedy.

Plutus, the Greek deity of wealth, guards the Fourth Circle of the Inferno. Countless scholars have attempted to interpret his shout of "Pape Satan, pape Satan aleppe", but even now, there is no true consensus.

The following are several theories:

  • "Oh, Satan, o Satan, god, king!"
  • "Oh Satan, oh Satan, God"
  • "The door of Satan, the door of Satan, proceed downward!"
  • "No peace, Satan! No peace, Satan! To the sword!"

The River Styx flows through the Circle of Wrath, and the wrathful are submerged in its foul waters. The boatman Phlegyas ferries Dante and Virgil across the river, only to be confronted by Filippo Argenti. Before he is able to harm Dante, the other wrathful souls drag him back into the muck.

The Circle of Heresy punishes its inhabitants by enclosing them within flaming tombs. Here, Dante encounters some fellow Florentines and even briefly discusses the politics of Florence with them before continuing onward once again.

The Seventh Circle, the Circle of Violence, is divided into three rings. In the first ring, the ring of Violence Against Neighbors, sinners boil in the Phlegethon, the river of blood and fire. The second ring, the Wood of Suicides, houses the Violent Against Self, those who attempted or committed suicide. The third Ring of Violence, that of Violence Against God, Art, and Nature is a Plain of Burning Sand, where the Blasphemers (the Violent against God) lie on top of the scorching sand, the Sodomites (the Violent against Nature) run in circles, and the Usurers (the Violent against Art) huddle together crying.

The Eighth Circle is made up of ten bolge, trenches filled with sinners who committed fraud. The first of these bolge houses the panderers and seducers, who are made to endlessly march as horned demons whip them mercilessly. The second bolgia contains the flatterers, who are immersed in feces. The simoniacs of the third bolgia are stuck face-down in holes, their feet sticking out as fire burns their soles. Down in the fourth bolgia, the sorcerers are forced to walk backwards, their heads twisted around 180° as punishment for seeking to view the future. In the fifth bolgia, corrupt politicians boil in a lake of tar, and the Malebranche demons tear them apart if they dare to emerge from the surface of the boiling, sticky liquid.

The sixth bolgia houses all of the hypocrites, made to walk a narrow path while weighed down by oppressive, leaden robes. The thieves of the seventh bolgia are imprisoned in a pit of venomouse reptiles. In the eighth bolgia, false counselors are trapped within flames. Almost to the bottom of the Eighth Circle, the Sowers of Discord are eternally mutilated and slashed apart by a demon's sword. In the deepest bolgia, the tenth, the Falsifiers are afflicted with disease, screaming, grime, and darkness.

Deep in the center of the Inferno, everything slows to a halt. The Ninth Circle, that of Treachery, is a frozen wasteland, where the tortured souls are trapped within the icy landscape.

The Ninth Circle is divided into four Rounds: the first of which is Caina—after Cain, the first murderer—is the eternal prison of Traitors to their Kindred. They are encased in ice from the neck down. The second Round is Antenora, after the Trojan soldier Antenor who betrayed Troy to the Greeks, for Traitors to their Country. Those imprisoned here have only their heads above the ice, unable to move their necks. The penultimate Round, Ptolomaea, houses the Traitors to their Guests. These pathetic souls are not even afforded the ability to cry, as their tears freeze in their sockets.

The deepest Round is Judecca, named for Judas Iscariot, betrayer of Jesus Christ. This is the place where Traitors to their Lords face eternal torment. They are entirely sealed within the ice, contorted in agonizing positions. In the center of it all is the Adversary, the Opposer, the Devil—Satan himself. Sealed in the ice from the waist up, with each beat of his wings blowing a gust of cold air against the sinners of the Ninth Circle, the three mouths of the Devil's three faces chew forever on three most heinous sinners: Marcus Junius Brutus, Gaius Cassius Longinus, and Judas Iscariot.

Canto IV


Abandon all hope, you who enter

I – Limbo

  • Virgil
  • Homer
  • Horace
  • Ovid
  • Lucan
  • Electra
  • Hector
  • Aeneas
  • Julius Caesar
  • Camilla
  • Saladin
  • Aristotle
  • Euclid
  • Ptolemy
  • Orpheus
  • Linus
  • Plato
  • Socrates
  • Cicero
  • Seneca
  • Hippocrates

Canto V


those condemned to this torment
were the souls of the lustful who put
rational thought below carnal desire

II – Lust

  • Achilles
  • Dido
  • Cleopatra
  • Helen of Troy
  • Paris
  • Francesca da Rimini
  • Semiramis (legends
    based on Shammuramat)

Canto VI


the Third Circle,
the circle of endless, cursed,
cold, and heavy rain

III – Gluttony

  • Cerberus
  • Ciacco

Canto VII


Pape Satàn, pape Satàn aleppe

IV – Greed

  • Plutus

Cantos VIII & IX


the souls of those whom wrath overcame

V – Wrath

  • Phlegyas, Guardian
    of the Fifth Circle
  • Filippo Argenti
  • Megaera
  • Allecto
  • Tisiphone
  • Medusa

Cantos X & XI


Here the great heretics grieve
with their followers from every sect.

VI – Heresy

  • Farinata
  • Cavalcante de' Cavalcanti
  • Epicurus
  • Emperor Frederick II

Cantos XII-XVII


The bed of the stream gets deeper and deeper
until it makes a circle and comes around
to the place where tyranny groans forever.

VII – Violence

Against Neighbors

  • Minotaur
  • Chiron
  • Nessus
  • Alexander the Great
  • Dionysius
  • Ezzelino III da Romano
  • Obizzo d'Este
  • Guy de Montfort
  • Attila the Hun
  • Sextus

Against Self

  • Pietro della Vigna
  • Lano da Siena
  • Jacopo Sant' Andrea

Against God, Art, & Nature

  • Capaneus
  • Brunetto Latini
  • Priscian
  • Francesco d'Accorso
  • Andrea de' Mozzi
  • Iacopo Rusticucci
  • Guido Guerra
  • Ciappo Ubriachi
  • Tegghiaio Aldobrandi
  • Catello di Rosso
    Gianfigliazzi
  • Reginaldo degli
    Scrovegni
  • Vitaliano di Iacopo
  • Giovanni
    di Buiamonte

Cantos XVIII-XXXI


Silent, alone, without company,
we walked on, one ahead, the other behind

VIII – Fraud

Panderers & Seducers

  • Jason
  • Venedico Caccianemico

Flatterers

  • Thais
  • Alessio Interminei

Simoniacs

  • Pope Nicholas III
  • Pope Boniface VIII
  • Pope Clement V

Sorcerors

  • King Amphiaraus
  • Tiresias
  • Eurypylus
  • Michael Scot
  • Guido Bonatti

Barrators

  • Friar Gomita
  • Michele Zanche

Hypocrites

  • Loderingo degli Andalò
  • Caiaphas

Thieves

  • Vanni Fucci
  • Cacus
  • Agnello Brunelleschi

Counsellors of Fraud

  • Ulysses (Odysseus)
  • Diomedes
  • Guido da Montefeltro

Sowers of Discord

  • Muhammad
  • Ali ibn Abi Talib
  • Pier da Medicina
  • Gaius Scribonius Curio
  • Mosca dei Lamberti
  • Bertran de Born

Falsifiers

  • Griffolino d'Arezzo
  • Capocchio
  • Gianni Schicchi de' Cavalcanti
  • Myrrha
  • Master Adam of Brescia
  • Zuleikha (Potiphar's wife)
  • Sinon

Cantos XXXII-XXXIV


I did not die, and yet I was no longer alive.
Imagine, if you can, what I became,
deprived of death and bereft of life.

IX – Treachery

Caina

  • Cain
  • Mordred
  • Alessandro and
    Napoleone degli Alberti
  • Vanni de' Cancellieri
  • Sassol Mascheroni

Antenora

  • Antenor
  • Bocca degli Abati
  • Buoso da Duera
  • Tesauro dei Beccheria
  • Gianni de' Soldanieri
  • Ganelon
  • Tebaldello de' Zambrasi
  • Count Ugolino
  • Archbishop Ruggieri

    Ptolomaea

  • Ptolemy son of Abubus
  • Fra Alberigo
  • Branca d'Oria

    Judecca

  • Judas Iscariot
  • Marcus Junius Brutus
  • Gaius Cassius Longinus
  • Satan/Lucifer
